guideline_references: GESA (Gastroenterological Society of Australia) — Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease guidance
NHFA/CSANZ Acute Coronary Syndrome guidance (chest-pain exclusion pathway)
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source is a physically-present after-hours clinician who performed an on-site 12-lead ECG to safely exclude acute coronary syndrome and then diagnosed reflux with a Low risk level and a routine 4-week GP review. Breath-Ezy CANNOT perform an ECG or examine the patient — the AI-correct answer is to recognise that acute chest pain waking a patient from sleep cannot be attributed to reflux remotely, and to arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment with an ECG, NOT to reassure and defer to a 4-week review.

Red-herring / dual-direction trap: the classic reflux tetrad (retrosternal burning, acid regurgitation, worse supine, relief with antacids) invites premature closure on a benign diagnosis, while the patient's own fear of a heart attack invites over-anchoring on cardiac. The safe telehealth answer sits between them: cannot exclude cardiac remotely, so uplift to urgent same-day in-person + ECG, but no active patient-reportable time-critical feature is present, so do NOT over-escalate to 000.

T4/T5 ceiling discipline: the in-person clinician's ability to call this Low risk depended on an ECG the AI cannot obtain; that ECG is exactly what a T3 urgent same-day in-person referral compensates for. Absent a patient-reportable time-critical feature (crushing/radiating pain, cold sweat, breathlessness, syncope), the correct telehealth tier is T3, not T4/T5.

Age and sex are not stated in the source note and are inferred (adult, ~54, male) from the social context (lives alone, large late high-fat meal, alcohol, Wt 92 kg) — flagged for clinician confirmation.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-025.

digital_tablet_anchoring.amt_version: AMT — Australian Medicines Terminology (current at build date 2026-07-18)
